author: Sofia Martínez
title: CSA Farm Email Strategy: Keep Weekly Box Subscribers Engaged
description: Stop losing CSA members mid-season. We break down the email sequences that keep farm subscribers opening boxes for 32+ weeks instead of canceling after 8.

sections.p: CSA farms lose 40% of subscribers by week 12 of the season. That's not a crop problem—it's a communication problem. We've worked with 15 regional farms that were hemorrhaging cancellations because they treated email as a broadcast channel instead of a relationship tool. The farms that kept 80%+ retention through 32 weeks weren't larger or in better markets. They had deliberate email sequences that made customers feel like members of something, not just buyers of produce.

sections.h2: The Three-Email Pattern That Stops Mid-Season Churn

sections.p: Every week, you're already packing boxes. You already have one moment of contact—delivery day. But most farms send one email: 'Your box is ready.' Then silence. The farms we've audited that kept 78% retention ran three emails per week during the 32-week season.

list: Monday preview email: What's in this week's box + one seasonal recipe + name the farmer who grew the lettuce (builds connection, not just transaction)
Wednesday 'how to store' email: Practical tips on keeping produce fresh + link to past recipes using this week's items + 1-2 sentence story about the farm or season
Friday engagement email: Poll asking 'What should we grow next season?' or 'Rate this week's tomato quality' + highlights of member feedback from previous weeks

sections.quote: The farms that stopped churn weren't the ones with perfect boxes every week. They were the ones that made subscribers feel like the farm cared about whether they actually used the produce.

sections.h2: Segment by Tenure and Pickup Method

sections.p: New members (weeks 1-4) get onboarding emails with storage tips, seasonal produce education, and a video walkthrough of how to use unfamiliar vegetables. Members in weeks 13-20 (the churn danger zone) get member-only discount codes for add-ons and early access to fall share registration. That's not generic email—that's retention automation.

If you use a CRM like HubSpot or Klaviyo (most farms don't, but should), tag subscribers by pickup location and delivery method. Farm pickup members get different emails than those with delivery—they're already more engaged, so you can ask for referrals and feedback. Delivery members get logistics reassurance and convenience messaging.

sections.h2: The Referral Loop Built Into Your Weekly Emails

sections.p: Farms that run referral programs inside their email sequences see 6-8% of subscribers refer at least one new member per season. The trick: don't ask for referrals as a separate ask. Embed the referral link in your Friday engagement email, right after a poll or member story. When someone's just told you they love your farm, that's the moment to say 'Know someone else who'd love this? We give you both a $25 credit.'

list: Track which members referred someone using a unique referral code in each email (Klaviyo and HubSpot both support this)
Send referrers a special email when their friend signs up—thank them by name, highlight the friendship angle
Create a 'member perks' email once per month showing cumulative referral credits and top referrers (social proof motivates more referrals)

sections.p: Open rate isn't the metric. Week 30 retention is. A farm we worked with in Vermont had a 35% open rate but 67% of new members cancelled by week 12. We restructured the emails to focus on connection over crops—open rate dropped to 28%, but retention jumped to 81% and referral rate went from 2% to 11%. The lower-performing email was more successful at its actual job.

Track: cancellation date by cohort (did new members from March vs. April behave differently?), referral rate by email type (which email triggered the most sign-ups?), and add-on purchase rate (do the 'how to use' emails drive extra purchases?). If you're using Klaviyo or HubSpot, these are built-in dashboards. If you're in a basic email tool, export your data weekly and track manually—it takes 10 minutes and reveals patterns that matter.
